name: Living Botanicals
description:
MAP's sculpture courtyard transforms into a garden with Living Botanicals — a space where plants native to the subcontinent grow, change, and are cared for over time.

Join us for a guided walk led by architect Bhavana Kumar of Kumar La Noce and urban gardener Kush Sethi as they share the process and vision behind this living installation.

name: DIY Asalato Instrument Making
description:
Image Credits: Siddharth Venkat 
Join us for a hands-on, immersive workshop where participants will craft their own Asalato—a traditional West African percussion instrument and learn how to play it. Guided step by step, participants will be introduced to the materials, techniques, and rhythms that bring this unique instrument to life.
The session also offers a glimpse into the cultural histories and stories behind the Asalato, alongside a brief exploration of indigenous sounds from across the world. Through group exercises and playful musical interactions, participants will experience the joy of collective rhythm-making, culminating in a fun, informal performance.
This workshop will include a guided walk of Beneath the Turning Sky.
No prior experience is required.
Participants are encouraged to bring materials like small pebbles, and other items that can be incorporated into the instrument.

name: Paper Gardens: Deaf-led
description:
Experience Paper Gardens: Art, Botany and Empire through a Deaf-led walkthrough that reimagines how we engage with botanical art.

This guided tour invites Deaf audiences to engage with the exhibition’s exploration of botanical illustrations created in India between the 17th and 19th centuries, and the complex histories of colonialism, labour, and knowledge embedded within them. These works were not merely aesthetic studies of plants, but part of a larger scientific project that documented native species for their potential medicinal and commercial value within imperial economies. 

Led by Akanksha Kamble from the MAP team , the walkthrough offers an inclusive, visual-first encounter with the exhibition, opening up conversations around art and ecology.

This programme is in conjunction with the ongoing exhibition, Paper Gardens: Art Botany and Empire.

‘Paper Gardens’ marks the continuation of a sustained, years-long collaboration between MAP and Impart — an online platform fostering greater public engagement with the art and cultural histories of South Asia.

name: Painting with Botanical Pigments
description:
Image credits: Sudha Palepu
Join us for a mellow, beginner-friendly pigment-making session on extracting colours from botanical sources. This is an opportunity to slow down, take a close look at colours in their natural form, and witness them transform on paper! During this workshop, participants will learn to extract inks and pigments from select botanical sources using simple techniques.

There will be demonstrations of these techniques, followed by guided hands-on colour-making by everyone attending. Upon making the colours, each participant will get to paint with the extracted inks and pigments on paper and take home their artworks at the end of the session.

This event is in conjunction with Paper Gardens: Art, Botany, and Empire.

‘Paper Gardens’ marks the continuation of a sustained, years-long collaboration between MAP and Impart — an online platform fostering greater public engagement with the art and cultural histories of South Asia.

name: Decoding Nature Through Journaling
description:
John Keats rightly said, “A thing of beauty is a joy for ever.” The beauty of the natural world endures and inspires us through its quiet resilience. 

Discover the beauty in nature's details through this nature journaling workshop led by Shilpashree, a Bengaluru-based visual artist.

name: Tracing the Living Line
description:
Image Credits: Hervé Perdriolle
 MAP invites you to an evening marking the launch of Hervé Perdriolle’s new book — the first publication devoted entirely to the artwork of artist  Jivya Soma Mashe (1934–2018).

A seminal figure of the Warli community in Maharashtra, Mashe transformed a ritual painting tradition into a powerful contemporary visual language. As Perdriolle suggests, he belongs to a lineage of artists — or “authors,” in Jean Dubuffet’s sense — whose works expand how we understand culture itself, revealing its depth, plurality, and imagination.

During the event, curator and art critic Perdriolle, will introduce the publication and speak about Mashe’s practice, with a special focus on a work currently on view in MAP’s exhibition Beneath the Turning Sky. 

The evening will conclude with a book signing and an opportunity to meet the author.

name: Evolving Practices in Architectural Heritage: Study, Making, and Meaning
description:
Image credits: The exterior façade of the University of Mumbai's Convocation Hall, framed by it's historic Gothic architecture. Courtesy Somaya Sampat (2015)
Join us for a moderated panel on the documentation, study, and stewardship of built environments. 

Architects, conservationists, and researchers frequently encounter layered histories — not only within buildings and cities, but also within the materials, skills, and artisanal knowledge that shape them. This discussion examines how conservation, adaptive reuse, and heritage documentation inform professional intervention and public experiences. It also reflects on the influence of institutional frameworks such as UNESCO, the Archaeological Survey of India, and other heritage bodies on research methodologies and design approaches.

By situating built heritage at the intersection of history, knowledge systems, governance, and contemporary design practice, the conversation invites a deeper engagement with how historic environments are interpreted and reimagined today.

This conversation has been organised by Impart — an online platform fostering greater public engagement with the art and cultural histories of South Asia.

name: Letters Across Time
description:
Letters Across Time is a reflective writing workshop that unfolds in dialogue with Beneath the Turning Sky. Beginning with a slow, attentive walk through the exhibition, participants are invited to notice, linger, and respond. From there, we write across time — toward imagined futures and back toward the memories that shape us. Through guided prompts, we explore hope, care, and the ways we choose to show up for one another and for the shared world we inhabit. Midway, the group comes together to imagine a small ritual from the future — something tender, slightly strange, and deeply human. The workshop closes with an anonymous exchange of letters, so each participant leaves carrying a fragment of someone else’s hope. A quiet, intimate space for reflection, imagination, and collective possibility.

The workshop begins at 2:00 pm, so we recommend having lunch before you arrive, and please bring your earphones as we’ll use them for a short music based reflection exercise.

name: Listening to Images
description:
Join this two-day workshop of creative inquiry and narrative experimentation where you engage with MAP’s latest exhibition, Beneath the Turning Sky. In the workshop, you will make your own short video works, responding to the exhibition through close looking, interpretation and poetic translation. Led by writer and sound researcher Mishika Goel, the workshop offers the space to reflect on how art can be re-encountered, re-imagined, and articulated through new narrative forms.

Drawing from her experiences in France and her creative process behind Prochaine Station: Aristide Briand, Mishika reflects on narrative as both method and medium. The workshop, situated at the intersection of language, moving image, and curatorial encounter, explores how artworks generate meaning beyond the visual- prompting stories, gestures, and varied forms of response..

This workshop is part of the ongoing series, Responding to Art through the Arts. Facilitated by arts practitioners, it aims to activate MAP’s galleries through a series of workshops that hold space for young people’s responses to artwork on display. 

It is conducted as part of our engagements for the MAP Youth Collective - a community for young people (18-25 year olds) to engage with the arts and culture sector. The collective is for, of and by young people — to explore our shared identities and interests through art.

Workshop requirements: Open to all skill levels, phone camera/professional camera can be used, along with a basic video-editing software on a PC/Mac.

name: See like a Curator: Paper Gardens
description:
Join us for a curator-led guided walk of the exhibition Paper Gardens: Art, Botany, and Empire.

Between the 17th and 20th centuries, the Indian subcontinent was the site of extensive botanical surveys under colonial rule. Plant collection and classification were closely tied to imperial economic, medical, and political interests, and botanical illustration played a central role in this project. Bringing together a new and historically significant collection of botanical art from this period, the exhibition draws on recent scholarship to recover the often uncredited identities and histories of the Indian artists who made them.

Shrey Maurya, curator of the exhibition and Research Director at Impart, leads this guided walk through the layered histories embedded in these artworks, and their relevance to how we understand the natural world today.

‘Paper Gardens’ marks the continuation of a sustained, years-long collaboration between MAP and Impart — an online platform fostering greater public engagement with the art and cultural histories of South Asia.

name: See like an Artist: Paper Gardens
description:
Experience the exhibition through the eyes of a botanical artist.

Led by Bangalore-based, self-taught artist Nirupa Rao, this walkthrough explores plants not as isolated specimens, but as dynamic, living beings embedded within rich ecological worlds. From strangler figs to basket ferns, Rao’s illustrations in the exhibition reveal trees as thriving micro-ecosystems — alive with birds, insects, and other forms of life that shape their survival.

This programme is in conjunction with the ongoing exhibition, Paper Gardens: Art Botany and Empire.

‘Paper Gardens’ marks the continuation of a sustained, years-long collaboration between MAP and Impart — an online platform fostering greater public engagement with the art and cultural histories of South Asia.

name: The Reserved Compartment: An Anti-Caste Love Poetry Showcase
description: Step into a space where voices that were long silenced, now echo with unapologetic power. The Reserved Compartment presents India's first Dalit-Ambedkarite anti-caste English Poetry Showcase—a bold, community-driven revolution redefining mainstream culture through raw, resonant verses and intimate storytelling. Curated for hearts ready to listen and minds eager to shift, this evening at MAP promises to dismantle barriers and ignite conversations that linger long after the lights dim. Join us in honouring resilience, nurturing love, reclaiming narratives, and co-creating a more inclusive tomorrow.

name: Wild Food Foraging Walk
description:
Image credits: Shruti Tharayil
Join Shruti Tharayil for a guided foraging walk and uncover the edible ecosystems hidden within our urban landscapes.

Curated by Forgotten Greens, Wild Food Walks are immersive community experiences that invite participants to reconnect with wild, edible plants growing all around us. The session also introduces 'Map the Wild'. This light strategy board game is set in a climate-altered city, where players navigate survival using wild edible and medicinal plants, and discover how traditional ecological knowledge can shape more resilient futures.

This programme is in conjunction with the  exhibition, Paper Gardens: Art Botany and Empire.

name: Sketch with Penciljam
description:
Whether you're a beginner, a seasoned artist, or simply curious, this sketching session led by Penciljam invites you to explore and draw from a living garden.

MAP's sculpture courtyard transforms into a garden with Living Botanicals — a space where plants native to the subcontinent grow, change, and are cared for over time.

name: Stories in Motion
description:
Got a story to tell? A lens to share? This one's for you.
For this session of Stories in Motion, we will be screening "Spectrum: Art & Space in Bengaluru"
The documentary explores the relationship between artists and the spaces they are allowed to occupy, questioning the divide between public and private spaces. It looks at what it means to live and work as an artist, alongside the perspectives of organisers and gatekeepers who manage these spaces. At its core, the film asks how accessible “public” space truly is, and who ultimately controls it.

This film screening series is by and for 18–25 year olds — an opportunity to share your work with a wider audience while engaging in thoughtful peer review and constructive feedback alongside fellow filmmakers in the making. If you’re a young filmmaker looking to test ideas, spark conversations, and build community, this event is for you!



This event is part of the Youth Engagement Series – Stories in Motion.

name: See like a Curator: Paper Gardens
description:
Join us for a curator-led guided walk of the exhibition Paper Gardens: Art, Botany, and Empire.

Between the 17th and 20th centuries, the Indian subcontinent was the site of extensive botanical surveys under colonial rule. Plant collection and classification were closely tied to imperial economic, medical, and political interests, and botanical illustration played a central role in this project. Bringing together a new and historically significant collection of botanical art from this period, the exhibition draws on recent scholarship to recover the often uncredited identities and histories of the Indian artists who made them.

Shrey Maurya, curator of the exhibition and Research Director at Impart, leads this guided walk through the layered histories embedded in these artworks, and their relevance to how we understand the natural world today.

Following the walkthrough, writer and poet Sumana Roy will read from her essay in the accompanying Paper Gardens book. Drawing on memory and lived experience, she reflects on the craze for the rhododendron and reconsiders the flower from within its native terrain.

‘Paper Gardens’ marks the continuation of a sustained, years-long collaboration between MAP and Impart — an online platform fostering greater public engagement with the art and cultural histories of South Asia.

name: Botanicals and the Book: A Look Inside the Paper Gardens Catalogue
description:
Didymocarpus evalifolia; Plate 1351, Icones Plantarum Indiae Orientalis, or Figures of Indian Plants, Volume IV; Author: Robert Wight; Artist: Govindoo; Lithographer: J. Dumphy; Lithograph; 1850. 
Courtesy Museum of Art & Photography (MAP), Bengaluru.
Developed alongside the exhibition of the same name, the Paper Gardens catalogue brings together over a hundred botanical illustrations from the 17th to the 20th centuries, tracing encounters between plants, artists, and institutions across continents and styles. Featuring essays by art historian Dr Holly Shaffer, botanist Dr Henry J. Noltie, and writer Sumana Roy, the book approaches botanical drawings as living documents.

Varun Nayar, Director of Impart and co-editor of the book, will be in conversation with the book’s designer, Tania Singh Khosla, to discuss the possibilities and challenges of working with botanical illustrations in book form.

Referencing her vast expertise with publication design — including past catalogues for MAP — Khosla will delve into the mechanics of working with such artworks; discuss the narrative and aesthetic choices that informed her approach; and reflect on how the book’s structure expands on the themes of the exhibition.

The exhibition, ‘Paper Gardens’, marks the continuation of a sustained, years-long collaboration between MAP and Impart — an online platform fostering greater public engagement with the art and cultural histories of South Asia.

name: Living Botanicals: Blind-led
description:
Join us for a blind-led walkthrough by Disability Rights Advocate and Assistant Professor Dr. Tony Kurian.

MAP's sculpture courtyard transforms into a garden with Living Botanicals — a space where plants native to the subcontinent grow, change, and are cared for over time.
